danger_signals.visual_form: Rival daimyo troops on map grid
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player fiefs; invasion risk

danger_signals.visual_form: Loyalty meter or population status indicator
danger_signals.meaning: Risk of rebellion; fief instability and loss of tax revenue

danger_signals.visual_form: Rice or gold counter critically low
danger_signals.meaning: Cannot sustain military or civilian expansion; vulnerability to economic collapse
Reduced combat effectiveness; units more likely to retreat or fail

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy faction non-aggression pact broken or marriage alliance severed
danger_signals.meaning: Former neutral/allied power now hostile; increased regional instability

safety_signals.visual_form: Loyalty meter in green or high numeric value
safety_signals.meaning: Stable fief; no rebellion risk; steady tax and resource flow

safety_signals.visual_form: Resource counter display at high levels
safety_signals.meaning: Economic stability; capacity for military expansion and training

safety_signals.visual_form: Army efficiency or training level indicator
safety_signals.meaning: Strong combat readiness; improved battle outcomes

safety_signals.visual_form: Alliance status indicator on map or faction display
safety_signals.meaning: Diplomatic security; reduced threat from that faction

safety_signals.visual_form: Fief ownership visualization with productivity markers
safety_signals.meaning: Stable economic base; sustainable regional power

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Historically associated with conquest and unification goal
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Rival daimyo displayed as opposing army units
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fiefs under Nobunaga control shown on strategic map

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Each selectable as player faction; historically distinct Japanese warlords
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Non-player daimyo displayed as enemy forces on map grid
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fief locations and troop placements indicate territorial control and regional power centers

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Early campaign (1560: Battle for the East / Daimyo Power Struggles)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple fragmented daimyo territories across map; no unified control
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must allocate resources carefully; military forces are smaller; diplomatic options (pacts, marriages) are primary tools

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Some territorial consolidation visible; stronger regional powers emerge
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increased military conflict; more fiefs controlled by fewer daimyo; player must balance expansion with defense

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Map shows dominant powers with clear territorial blocs; fewer independent daimyo
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Large-scale military campaigns; unification goal becomes achievable; final confrontations with major rivals

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Fief under player control with high productivity
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fief displays high tax collection and resource generation rates
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can allocate soldiers, invest in flood control, expand towns, recruit ninja

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Loyalty meter drops; peasant satisfaction low
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must give food/rice to peasants to restore loyalty; avoid raising taxes; risk of fief loss to rebellion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Battle map with terrain; turn-based grid movement
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Each turn = one day; forces move and engage; victory through retreat forcing, command unit destruction, outlasting, or supply exhaustion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Spy action selection in turn menu
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player gains information on rival daimyo strength; can influence enemy decisions covertly

environmental_storytelling.example: Town expansion, flood control improvements, and productivity changes visually demonstrate economic progress and player investment decisions
Strategic map shows which daimyo control which fiefs; color or symbol coding indicates faction ownership and regional power distribution
Rice and gold transfers between fiefs show economic interconnection; shortages or surpluses signal political stress and military capability
Troop counts and unit types (soldiers, ninja) at each fief communicate military readiness and threat level
Declining loyalty signals mismanagement, over-taxation, or crop failure; visual metric reflects fief stability

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Remain in fief; generate tax revenue; do not rebel
May rebel if taxes increased; leave fief; loyalty can be stolen by rival daimyo
Higher combat efficiency; better performance in battle; increased morale
Lower combat effectiveness; more likely to retreat; vulnerable to supply exhaustion
Will not initiate military action; diplomatic cooperation possible
Become hostile; initiate invasions; competitive for resources and territory
Temporary reduction in administrative capacity; affects decision-making options
Increased tax collection; higher peasant engagement; greater military recruitment capacity
Increased productivity long-term; immediate cost to peasant loyalty

notes: Nobunaga's Ambition (SNES, 1992) is a turn-based grand strategy simulation set during Japan's Sengoku period. Visual communication is primarily text-based and numeric (loyalty meters, resource counters, army efficiency ratings) rather than detailed graphics. World-state is communicated through strategic map visualization (fief ownership, troop positioning) and turn-based mechanics (seasonal turns for administration, daily turns for battle). Danger and safety are signaled through resource availability, peasant loyalty metrics, and military readiness indicators. Factions are differentiated by historical daimyo identity and territorial control rather than color-coding or distinctive visual sigils. Political situation (war, alliance, rebellion) is communicated through gameplay state transitions and menu options available to the player each turn.
